unit.name: Weight initialization for stable CNN training
unit.description: Shows why too-small or too-large initial weights destabilize activations and introduces Kaiming initialization as the practical default.
unit.summary: This segment explains weight initialization as the final step needed to make an architecture ready for training, with the central goal of keeping activation statistics stable across layers [ts=2531s][ts=2604s]. If weights are initialized too small, the mean and standard deviation of activations shrink toward zero as depth increases [ts=2565s][ts=2600s]. If initialized too large, activations grow layer by layer and quickly become unmanageable in very deep models [ts=2608s][ts=2637s]. The practical recipe presented is Kaiming/MSRA initialization, with scale sqrt(2 / D_in) for ReLU networks, which approximately preserves activation mean and standard deviation across depth [ts=2674s][ts=2736s]. The same principle applies to convolutional layers by taking D_in from the kernel dimensions and channel count [ts=2777s][ts=2796s].

key_points.text: Too-small weight initialization causes activation means and standard deviations to shrink toward zero in deeper layers.
Too-large weight initialization makes activations grow layer by layer and destabilizes deep networks.
Kaiming/MSRA initialization uses std = sqrt(2 / D_in) for ReLU-style networks.
The purpose of Kaiming initialization is to keep activation statistics relatively constant across layers.
For convolutional layers, D_in is computed from kernel height, kernel width, and input channels.

kp_catalog.name: Kaiming/MSRA initialization
kp_catalog.description: A ReLU-aware initialization rule that scales weights by sqrt(2 / D_in) to keep activation statistics stable through depth.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because kaiming/MSRA initialization clarifies a ReLU-aware initialization rule that scales weights by sqrt(2 / D_in) to keep activation statistics stable through depth, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
